content.skill:
# Architecture Explainer

## Summary

Explain a system or codebase architecture in a way that is grounded, digestible, and useful for future work.

## Why This Skill Matters

Architecture explanations fail when they either oversimplify into platitudes or drown readers in implementation detail. This skill matters when someone needs a mental model that is accurate enough to guide future changes.

## When To Use

- A system needs to be explained to new contributors or adjacent teams.
- You need an architecture explainer with system boundaries, component roles, flows, tradeoffs, and known complexity hotspots instead of unstructured notes.
- The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.

## Avoid When

- The task needs direct code execution rather than planning, review, or explanation.
- There is not enough repository or system context to support a grounded output.

## Inputs

- codebase or architecture artifacts
- audience
- system boundaries
- known pain points

## Output Contract

- architecture overview
- component map
- data or control flow summary
- key tradeoffs

## Workflow

1. Choose the explanation level for the audience before summarizing components.
2. Map the system boundaries, core flows, and critical dependencies.
3. Name the architectural tradeoffs and complexity hotspots that matter for future work.
4. Finish with a short glossary and open questions for follow-up review.

## Deliverable Shape

An architecture explainer with system boundaries, component roles, flows, tradeoffs, and known complexity hotspots

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Failure Modes To Avoid

- Turning code structure into an architecture explanation without showing runtime behavior.
- Explaining every component equally instead of focusing on leverage points and boundaries.
- Describing the current shape without naming the tradeoffs it imposes on future work.

## Maturity Note

This is a flagship Skill. It is intentionally stronger than the broader seeded library, but not automatically certified.

| Criterion | Strong | Weak |

content.edge_cases:
# Edge Cases

### Risk

The system has grown through pragmatism, migration residue, or local workarounds rather than clean intentional design.

Explain the shape honestly, distinguish intended design from accumulated reality, and show where the rough edges matter.

### Failure Probe

Romanticizes the current architecture and hides the accidental complexity.

## Documentation and code disagree

### Risk

Existing diagrams or docs describe a cleaner system than the code and runtime behavior reveal.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Use the live implementation as the primary reference, note the drift, and explain where legacy docs may still be useful.

### Failure Probe

Repeats the old diagram because it sounds better than the real system.

## Audience spans multiple technical levels

### Risk

The same explainer has to work for product, adjacent engineers, and new maintainers.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Layer the explanation from boundary summary to deeper flow detail and make the intended audience of each section explicit.

### Failure Probe

Picks one depth level and leaves the rest of the audience behind.

content.skill:
# Bug Triage

## Summary

Turn a raw bug report into a triage artifact with severity, repro confidence, likely owners, and next actions.

## Why This Skill Matters

Bug triage matters because the cost is not just mislabeling severity. Bad triage sends the wrong people into the wrong problem with the wrong urgency. This skill is for converting noisy reports into grounded next moves.

## When To Use

- An incoming bug report needs structured triage.
- You need a bug triage brief with severity rationale, repro assessment, likely surface area, and next-step ownership instead of unstructured notes.
- The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.

## Avoid When

- The task needs direct code execution rather than planning, review, or explanation.
- There is not enough repository or system context to support a grounded output.

## Inputs

- bug report
- logs or screenshots if available
- affected area
- user impact context

## Output Contract

- triage summary
- severity recommendation
- repro notes
- owner suggestions
- next actions

## Workflow

1. Separate observed symptoms, user impact, and speculation about cause.
2. Estimate severity and repro confidence from the evidence actually available.
3. Identify the likely surface area, owner group, and next debugging move.
4. Call out containment or rollback decisions when they materially matter.

## Deliverable Shape

A bug triage brief with severity rationale, repro assessment, likely surface area, and next-step ownership

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Failure Modes To Avoid

- Guessing root cause from a bug report and treating the guess like established fact.
- Equating loud stakeholder attention with actual user impact.
- Marking severity without noting repro confidence or containment urgency.

## Maturity Note

This is a flagship Skill. It is intentionally stronger than the broader seeded library, but not automatically certified.

# Rubric

| Criterion | Strong | Weak |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Evidence hygiene | Separates observed symptoms, user impact, and speculative cause cleanly. | Blends report content and engineer guesswork into one triage narrative. |
| Urgency judgment | Assigns urgency based on actual impact, blast radius, and containment needs. | Uses severity labels as shorthand without explaining the decision logic. |
| Next-step clarity | Leaves the team knowing who should look next, what to verify, and whether mitigation is needed immediately. | Creates a tidy ticket that still leaves the next move ambiguous. |

content.edge_cases:
# Edge Cases

## The report is credible but irreproducible

### Risk

Users are clearly hitting a problem, but the team cannot reproduce it in a controlled environment yet.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Keep impact and repro confidence separate, identify the missing diagnostic evidence, and define the fastest route to better signal.

### Failure Probe

Downgrades the issue solely because engineers cannot reproduce it on demand.

## Symptom spans multiple owners

### Risk

The visible failure could come from several services, clients, or infrastructure layers.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Map the plausible surface area, note the most likely owner groups, and keep the handoff logic explicit.

### Failure Probe

Throws the issue at the loudest or most obvious team without narrowing the surface area.

## Containment may matter more than diagnosis

### Risk

The right next step is rollback, feature disablement, or communication, not deeper root-cause analysis.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Call out containment decisions separately from debugging work and explain the tradeoff.

### Failure Probe

Treats triage as a purely investigative step and misses the immediate user-risk decision.

content.skill:
# Migration Planner

## Summary

Plan a code, data, or platform migration with dependencies, sequence, and rollback considerations.

## When To Use

- A system, library, or data migration needs a staged plan.
- You need a migration plan with phases, prerequisites, checkpoints, rollback paths, and ownership notes instead of unstructured notes.
- The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.

## Avoid When

- The task needs direct code execution rather than planning, review, or explanation.
- There is not enough repository or system context to support a grounded output.

## Inputs

- migration target
- current state
- constraints
- dependencies

## Output Contract

- migration plan
- dependency map
- risk list
- rollback notes

## Workflow

1. Confirm the decision context, audience, and missing inputs.
2. Build a migration plan with phases, prerequisites, checkpoints, rollback paths, and ownership notes using the available evidence and constraints.
3. Separate facts from assumptions and make confidence explicit.
4. Finish with next actions, open questions, or escalation notes.

## Deliverable Shape

A migration plan with phases, prerequisites, checkpoints, rollback paths, and ownership notes

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Maturity Note

This Skill is meaningfully filled for v1, with stronger examples and tests than the broader seeded tier.

content.skill:
# API Change Reviewer

## Summary

Review an API change for silent behavior shifts, consumer blast radius, and rollout realism.

## When To Use

- An API shape or default is changing and downstream consumers may break or behave differently.
- You need a review with compatibility analysis, hidden behavioral change detection, blast-radius reasoning, and rollout guidance instead of a code review summary.
- The organization repeats this pattern enough that the review logic should be reusable.

## Avoid When

- You only need schema linting or code-generation checks.
- The contract is still entirely private and there are no meaningful consumers yet.

## Inputs

- current API contract or behavior
- proposed change
- known consumers or consumer types
- rollout, deprecation, or migration plan
- known monitoring or support constraints

## Output Contract

- API review brief
- compatibility analysis
- consumer-risk map
- rollout and migration recommendations

## Workflow

1. Confirm the decision context, audience, and missing inputs.
2. Build an api change review with compatibility analysis, hidden behavioral changes, consumer blast radius, and rollout guidance using the available evidence and constraints.
3. Separate facts from assumptions and make confidence explicit.
4. Finish with next actions, open questions, or escalation notes.

## Deliverable Shape

An API change review with compatibility analysis, hidden behavioral changes, consumer blast radius, and rollout guidance

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Maturity Note

This Skill is meaningfully filled for v1, with stronger examples and tests than the broader seeded tier.
content.readme:
# API Change Reviewer

Review an API change for silent behavior shifts, consumer blast radius, and rollout realism.

## Quick View

- Category: `engineering`
- Maturity: `frontier`
- Tier: `developed`
- Evaluation status: `sampled`

## What This Skill Does

API changes fail less often because a field disappeared than because behavior, defaults, timing, or migration posture changed without a disciplined review of real consumers.

## When To Reach For It

- An API shape or default is changing and downstream consumers may break or behave differently.
- A seemingly small contract change could hide migration, deprecation, or observability risk.

## What You Should Provide

- current API contract or behavior
- proposed change
- known consumers or consumer types
- rollout, deprecation, or migration plan
- known monitoring or support constraints

## What You Should Get Back

- API review brief
- compatibility analysis
- consumer-risk map
- rollout and migration recommendations

## Repository Notes

- Main instructions live in `SKILL.md`.
- Machine-readable metadata lives in `skill.json`.
- Examples, rubric, edge cases, and tests live under `references/` and `tests/`.

## Quality Position

This Skill is meaningfully filled for v1, with stronger examples and tests than the broader seeded tier.
content.examples:
# Examples

## Public REST field deprecation

### Scenario

A public endpoint is dropping a field that external customers still read, and the team needs a safer migration plan than a release note line item.

### Prompt

Review the proposed deprecation of a public response field and summarize compatibility risk, likely consumer breakage, and the minimum safe migration path.

### Strong Response Should

- Separate hard breaking changes from behavior changes that are only likely to break some clients.
- Call out undocumented consumer assumptions such as nullability or default parsing.
- Recommend rollout and communication steps that match the blast radius.

## Pagination default change

### Scenario

An internal service wants to change pagination defaults to reduce load, but several downstream consumers may rely on current behavior.

### Prompt

Review the pagination default change for compatibility, consumer impact, and rollout sequencing.

### Strong Response Should

- Treat default behavior as part of the contract, not an implementation detail.
- Show which consumers are likely to feel the change first.
- Recommend a rollout path safer than flipping the default globally.

## Webhook retry semantics shift

### Scenario

A platform team wants to change retry timing and idempotency guidance for webhook delivery, and support is worried about duplicate downstream actions.

### Prompt

Review the webhook retry semantics change for contract risk, hidden consumer assumptions, and the safest migration posture.

### Strong Response Should

- Treat timing and retry behavior as contract, not implementation detail.
- Call out downstream systems likely to duplicate work or mis-handle retries.
- Tie the rollout plan to observability and support readiness.
content.rubric:
# Rubric

| Criterion | Strong | Weak |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Contract compatibility | Identifies explicit schema breaks and quieter behavioral shifts that consumers still experience as contract changes. | Looks only for obvious type or field removals. |
| Consumer blast radius | Maps which consumers are affected, how badly, and where assumptions are undocumented. | Talks about consumers generically without narrowing the impact. |
| Rollout realism | Pairs the review with deprecation, migration, communication, and support steps that match the risk. | Finds issues but leaves rollout strategy naive or missing. |

## Certification Note

Certified status requires benchmark coverage, score threshold, and `manual_review: passed`. It is not granted by structure alone.
content.edge_cases:
# Edge Cases

## Behavior changes without schema diffs

### Risk

Defaults, ordering, retry timing, or nullability semantics change even though the surface looks almost identical.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Treat behavior as contract and make the hidden breakage risk explicit.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## Some consumers are unknown

### Risk

The team cannot confidently enumerate all callers, making a clean migration story impossible to assume.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Lower confidence, recommend defensive rollout steps, and avoid pretending the blast radius is fully mapped.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## Versioning promises more safety than reality

### Risk

A version bump or alias exists, but migration guidance, support windows, and observability are still weak.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Review migration posture as seriously as the contract change itself and call out where versioning is mostly cosmetic.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

# Weekly Ops Update

## Summary

Build a weekly operating update that highlights signal, risk, and follow-ups without reading like status spam.

## Why This Skill Matters

Ops updates become useless when they read like activity logs. This skill matters when leaders need the operating signal of the week in a format they can scan, trust, and act on.

## When To Use

- A weekly operating update needs to be synthesized from many small inputs.
- You need a weekly ops update with signal summary, metrics shifts, risk callouts, owners, and next actions instead of unstructured notes.

content.skill:
# Executive Meeting Prep

## Summary

Prepare a leadership meeting brief with objectives, decisions, risks, and likely questions.

## Why This Skill Matters

Executive meeting prep fails when it optimizes for completeness instead of decision velocity. This skill matters when leaders need the minimum context required to choose, align, or challenge in the room.

## When To Use

- An executive meeting needs a concise prep brief.
- You need an executive meeting brief with objectives, decision points, supporting context, risks, and anticipated questions instead of unstructured notes.
- The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.

## Avoid When

- The update requires live operational data that has not been gathered.
- You need a human decision-maker to resolve a conflict before summarizing it.

## Inputs

- agenda
- supporting notes
- participants
- known tensions

## Output Contract

- meeting prep brief
- decision list
- risk notes
- question prep

## Workflow

1. Start from the decisions the meeting must produce, not the background material.
2. Compress context into the smallest set of facts leaders need before discussion.
3. Anticipate hard questions, dissent, and tradeoffs that will surface in the room.
4. Finish with meeting asks, owners, and follow-up checkpoints.

## Deliverable Shape

An executive meeting brief with objectives, decision points, supporting context, risks, and anticipated questions

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Failure Modes To Avoid

- Turning the prep note into a status document with no decision spine.
- Including background material that is interesting but not actionable in the meeting.
- Ignoring the questions executives will ask when assumptions, cost, or timing feel thin.

## Maturity Note

This is a flagship Skill. It is intentionally stronger than the broader seeded library, but not automatically certified.

### Failure Probe

Presents the meeting as one binary decision and hides the real decision stack.

## Leadership views are already split

### Risk

Different executives are likely to enter the room with conflicting priors or incentives.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Name the likely fault lines, prepare the supporting evidence for each, and show where alignment is and is not realistic.

### Failure Probe

Writes a neutral brief that ignores the actual conflict dynamics in the room.

## Decision must be made under incomplete data

### Risk

The team cannot gather all the desired evidence before the meeting date.

### How The Skill Should Respond

State the minimum evidence available, define the residual risk, and propose the least-regret decision posture.

### Failure Probe

Pretends the brief is complete rather than helping leaders decide under uncertainty.

content.skill:
# Operating Rhythm Designer

## Summary

Design an operating rhythm with clear decision cadence, artifact discipline, and escalation paths.

## When To Use

- The team has too many meetings, too little decision clarity, or too much status noise between leaders.
- You need a rhythm with cadence, artifacts, owners, and escalation paths instead of another calendar cleanup exercise.
- The operating problem repeats often enough that the coordination model itself should be reusable.

## Avoid When

- The organization is too early to define recurring decisions or owners at all.
- The actual problem is leadership conflict or missing authority, not cadence design.

## Inputs

- org shape and key functions
- recurring decisions that need cadence support
- metrics and reporting availability
- existing meetings or rituals
- known escalation failures

## Output Contract

- operating rhythm design
- meeting and artifact cadence map
- owner assignments
- escalation rules

## Workflow

1. Confirm the decision context, audience, and missing inputs.
2. Build an operating rhythm with decision cadence, recurring artifacts, meeting ownership, and escalation paths using the available evidence and constraints.
3. Separate facts from assumptions and make confidence explicit.
4. Finish with next actions, open questions, or escalation notes.

## Deliverable Shape

An operating rhythm with decision cadence, recurring artifacts, meeting ownership, and escalation paths

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Maturity Note

This Skill is meaningfully filled for v1, with stronger examples and tests than the broader seeded tier.

content.examples:
# Examples

## Founder-led startup scaling up

### Scenario

A startup is moving from founder-driven coordination to a functional leadership team and wants fewer surprises without adding bureaucracy for its own sake.

### Prompt

Design an operating rhythm for a scaling startup with weekly execution reviews, monthly planning, and explicit escalation handling across product, engineering, and GTM.

### Strong Response Should

- Define which decisions belong in which cadence rather than stacking more meetings.
- Specify the artifact each forum requires to stay useful.
- Make escalation paths explicit when a decision cannot wait for the next scheduled review.

## Distributed engineering and product cadence

### Scenario

A distributed organization has metrics arriving late, duplicated reviews, and recurring disagreement about where prioritization decisions should happen.

### Prompt

Create an operating rhythm that reduces duplicated forums, clarifies decision ownership, and matches meeting cadence to data freshness.

### Strong Response Should

- Match cadence to how quickly meaningful signal actually arrives.
- Collapse redundant forums instead of renaming them.
- Clarify who decides, who prepares the artifact, and when escalation is triggered.

## Post-incident operating reset

### Scenario

After several avoidable escalations, leadership wants to redesign the weekly operating system so risks surface earlier and owners do not improvise under pressure.

### Prompt

Design an operating rhythm that improves weekly signal review, issue escalation, and owner discipline after repeated cross-functional misses.

### Strong Response Should

- Tie the rhythm to the specific operating failures that keep recurring.
- Define what gets surfaced proactively versus what waits for escalation.
- Avoid creating a ceremony-heavy model that nobody can sustain.
content.rubric:
# Rubric

| Criterion | Strong | Weak |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Decision cadence fit | Matches each recurring forum to the type and speed of decisions it should support. | Creates calendar structure without improving decision timing. |
| Artifact discipline | Names the artifact, owner, and expected level of preparation required for each cadence point. | Treats meetings as sufficient without defining the inputs that make them useful. |
| Escalation clarity | Shows what gets escalated, to whom, and on what trigger instead of relying on improvisation. | Mentions escalation but leaves the actual route ambiguous. |

## Certification Note

Certified status requires benchmark coverage, score threshold, and `manual_review: passed`. It is not granted by structure alone.
content.edge_cases:
# Edge Cases

## Metrics arrive too late for the proposed cadence

### Risk

A meeting cadence looks disciplined on paper, but the underlying data is not fresh enough to support it.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Match review frequency to signal freshness and avoid rituals that only recycle stale information.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## Too many parallel review forums already exist

### Risk

New cadence design piles onto legacy meetings instead of replacing them, creating more overhead without more clarity.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Treat forum removal as part of the design and collapse redundant rituals directly.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## No stable owner exists for a key artifact

### Risk

The operating system depends on a recurring artifact that nobody reliably owns or updates.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Call out the ownership gap explicitly and avoid pretending cadence alone will solve it.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

content.skill:
# Launch Plan Generator

## Summary

Build a practical launch plan with sequencing, owners, messaging dependencies, and readiness checks.

## Why This Skill Matters

Launches fail when teams confuse a task list with a launch plan. This skill matters when sequencing, readiness gates, owner handoffs, and post-launch monitoring actually need to line up across functions.

## When To Use

- A launch needs a coordinated GTM plan across functions.
- You need a launch plan with sequencing, owners, deliverables, decision gates, and post-launch follow-up instead of unstructured notes.
- The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.

## Avoid When

- There is no defined audience, channel, or launch moment yet.
- You need final legal review or brand approval rather than a working draft.

## Inputs

- launch objective
- audience
- timeline
- channels
- constraints

## Output Contract

- launch plan
- owner map
- dependency list
- readiness checkpoints

## Workflow

1. Anchor the plan in launch objective, audience, and non-negotiable constraints.
2. Map workstreams, dependencies, and owner handoffs in time order.
3. Call out readiness gates and the decisions required before launch day.
4. End with post-launch checks and a short contingency plan.

## Deliverable Shape

A launch plan with sequencing, owners, deliverables, decision gates, and post-launch follow-up

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Failure Modes To Avoid

- Creating a long list of launch tasks with no critical path or decision gates.
- Treating messaging, product, support, and analytics as parallel workstreams with no dependency logic.
- Ending the plan at launch time instead of defining what happens after release.

## Maturity Note

This is a flagship Skill. It is intentionally stronger than the broader seeded library, but not automatically certified.

content.rubric:
# Rubric

| Criterion | Strong | Weak |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Dependency logic | Maps the critical path, decision gates, and cross-functional dependencies clearly enough to run the launch. | Looks comprehensive while hiding what actually blocks what. |
| Operational realism | Reflects ownership, review cycles, and readiness checks teams really need before launch day. | Reads like a generic launch checklist that could apply to anything. |
| Post-launch preparedness | Includes monitoring, support posture, and contingency handling for the first days after release. | Treats launch as the finish line and ignores the immediate aftermath. |

content.edge_cases:
# Edge Cases

## Launch date is fixed but dependencies are not

### Risk

The team has a public date committed while several gating decisions or workstreams remain unstable.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Make the hard gates explicit, show contingency branches, and force visibility into what would trigger a scope cut or delay.

### Failure Probe

Produces a confident timeline that assumes every dependency resolves on schedule.

## One cross-functional team is the bottleneck

### Risk

Legal, analytics, support, or enablement work is likely to constrain the entire launch.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Center the bottleneck in the plan, identify parallelizable work around it, and clarify what can and cannot proceed without it.

### Failure Probe

Treats all workstreams as equally paced and hides the actual blocking team.

## Post-launch support load is uncertain

# Positioning Statement Builder

- alternatives

content.skill:
# Sales Call Brief

## Summary

Prepare an account-specific call brief with stakeholder intent, likely objections, and the best next move.

## When To Use

- A high-stakes prospect, renewal, or escalation call needs sharper prep than the CRM notes provide.
- You need a brief with account context, stakeholder goals, likely objections, proof priorities, and follow-up ownership instead of scattered notes.
- The organization repeats this pre-call work enough that the posture should be reusable.

## Avoid When

- You have almost no account context and need discovery questions more than a brief.
- The meeting is low stakes and does not justify a custom prep artifact.

## Inputs

- account context and stage
- recent activity or call notes
- meeting objective
- known stakeholders and risks
- available proof points or weak spots

## Output Contract

- sales call brief
- stakeholder-specific talk track priorities
- objection prep
- follow-up plan

## Workflow

1. Confirm the decision context, audience, and missing inputs.
2. Build a sales call brief with account context, stakeholder goals, objection handling posture, proof priorities, and follow-up ownership using the available evidence and constraints.
3. Separate facts from assumptions and make confidence explicit.
4. Finish with next actions, open questions, or escalation notes.

## Deliverable Shape

A sales call brief with account context, stakeholder goals, objection handling posture, proof priorities, and follow-up ownership

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Maturity Note

This Skill is meaningfully filled for v1, with stronger examples and tests than the broader seeded tier.
content.readme:
# Sales Call Brief

Prepare an account-specific call brief with stakeholder intent, likely objections, and the best next move.

## Quick View

- Category: `gtm`
- Maturity: `frontier`
- Tier: `developed`
- Evaluation status: `sampled`

## What This Skill Does

Teams often overprepare on product facts and underprepare on stakeholder dynamics, proof quality, and the one move most likely to change the conversation.

## When To Reach For It

- A high-stakes prospect, renewal, or escalation call needs sharper prep than the CRM notes provide.
- The account history is noisy enough that the team needs one grounded pre-call artifact before walking in.

## What You Should Provide

- account context and stage
- recent activity or call notes
- meeting objective
- known stakeholders and risks
- available proof points or weak spots

## What You Should Get Back

- sales call brief
- stakeholder-specific talk track priorities
- objection prep
- follow-up plan

## Repository Notes

content.examples:
# Examples

## Renewal rescue call

### Scenario

A large renewal is at risk because adoption is uneven and an executive sponsor is questioning expansion value.

### Prompt

Prepare a renewal rescue call brief that sharpens the customer context, likely objections, and the strongest conversation path for the account team.

### Strong Response Should

- Frame the real deal risk instead of just summarizing the account record.
- Anticipate executive and operator objections separately.
- Recommend the next-best conversation move rather than a generic pitch sequence.

## Security review call

### Scenario

A late-stage deal hinges on a security and procurement conversation with both technical and business stakeholders present.

### Prompt

Create a sales call brief for a security review meeting with likely objections, proof priorities, and follow-up ownership.

### Strong Response Should

- Separate stakeholder goals instead of treating the audience as one buyer.
- Match proof points to the specific objections likely to surface.
- Leave the team with a practical follow-up plan if the meeting stalls.

## Expansion call with skeptical operator

### Scenario

An expansion opportunity depends on an operator who likes the product but distrusts the implementation burden and internal change cost.

### Prompt

Build a call brief for the expansion meeting that accounts for operator skepticism, executive upside, and the most credible proof sequence.

### Strong Response Should

- Treat implementation burden as a central objection, not a side note.
- Balance executive value framing with operator-level credibility.
- Recommend a conversation move that earns the next step instead of overselling the expansion.
content.rubric:
# Rubric

| Criterion | Strong | Weak |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Account specificity | Feels tailored to the actual account, stage, and stakeholder mix rather than reusable sales wallpaper. | Could describe almost any customer call. |
| Objection anticipation | Names the likely objections and the proof or posture each one requires. | Mentions risk vaguely without preparing the team for the actual pushback. |
| Conversation leverage | Improves what the team should do in the meeting and immediately after it. | Summarizes context without changing the conversation strategy. |

## Certification Note

Certified status requires benchmark coverage, score threshold, and `manual_review: passed`. It is not granted by structure alone.
content.edge_cases:
# Edge Cases

## CRM data is stale or contradictory

### Risk

The record includes outdated notes, mixed signals, or inconsistent stage reporting.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Flag stale context, separate high-confidence facts from softer signals, and avoid overfitting the brief to bad CRM hygiene.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## Stakeholders have conflicting incentives

### Risk

The economic buyer, operator, and security reviewer want different things from the call.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Keep the stakeholder goals distinct and avoid flattening the conversation into one generic talk track.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## Proof is weaker than the team wants

### Risk

The account team wants to lean on claims or references that are not actually strong enough for the objection at hand.

### How The Skill Should Respond

Downgrade weak proof, suggest safer supporting evidence, and keep the brief from promising what the team cannot substantiate live.

### Failure Probe

The response ignores the complication or hides the uncertainty instead of adapting the workflow.

## When To Use

- The team needs customer proof for messaging or sales enablement.
- You need a proof library with evidence statements, quote candidates, and relevance by audience instead of unstructured notes.
- The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.

## Avoid When

- There is no defined audience, channel, or launch moment yet.
- You need final legal review or brand approval rather than a working draft.

## Inputs

- calls or interviews
- customer stories
- target use cases

## Output Contract

- proof point set
- quote candidates
- usage notes

## Workflow

1. Confirm the decision context, audience, and missing inputs.
2. Build a proof library with evidence statements, quote candidates, and relevance by audience using the available evidence and constraints.
3. Separate facts from assumptions and make confidence explicit.
4. Finish with next actions, open questions, or escalation notes.

## Deliverable Shape

A proof library with evidence statements, quote candidates, and relevance by audience

## Guardrails

- Do not invent facts that are not present in the supplied materials.
- Make uncertainty explicit when inputs are incomplete or contradictory.
- Keep the output structured enough that another operator can reuse it.

## Maturity Note

This Skill is part of the seeded breadth layer. It is intentionally lighter than the developed and flagship tiers.
